expanded_description: Dominant and recessive inheritance patterns describe how alleles interact to determine trait expression in offspring. In simple Mendelian inheritance, dominant alleles mask the expression of recessive alleles in heterozygous individuals, while recessive traits only appear when an individual inherits two recessive alleles. Understanding these patterns enables prediction of trait inheritance across generations.

core_concept.principle: Dominant alleles express their trait in both homozygous and heterozygous conditions, while recessive alleles only express their trait in homozygous recessive conditions.
core_concept.explanation: When an individual inherits different alleles for a gene (heterozygous), the dominant allele determines the observable trait (phenotype), while the recessive allele remains hidden but can be passed to offspring. Recessive traits only appear when an individual inherits two copies of the recessive allele (homozygous recessive). This pattern explains why traits can skip generations and reappear in later generations.

image.alt: Diagram showing dominant and recessive allele expression in homozygous and heterozygous individuals
image.caption: Figure 9.7: Dominant alleles (capital letters) express in both homozygous dominant and heterozygous conditions; recessive alleles (lowercase) only express in homozygous recessive conditions.

sections.introduction: Dominant and recessive inheritance represents the simplest pattern of genetic inheritance, first described by Gregor Mendel through his pea plant experiments. This pattern applies to traits controlled by a single gene with two alleles where one allele completely masks the expression of the other in heterozygous individuals. Understanding these patterns is fundamental to predicting how traits pass from parents to offspring.

key_concept.definition: Dominant and recessive inheritance describes the relationship between alleles where the dominant allele masks expression of the recessive allele in heterozygous individuals, with recessive traits only appearing in homozygous recessive genotypes.
key_concept.context: Applies to simple Mendelian traits controlled by single genes with complete dominance; does not apply to incomplete dominance, codominance, or polygenic traits covered in Section 9.8.

image.alt: Comparison of dominant and recessive allele expression patterns
image.caption: Dominant alleles determine phenotype in heterozygotes; recessive alleles require homozygous condition for expression.

classifications.value: Alleles that express their trait in both homozygous dominant (AA) and heterozygous (Aa) genotypes
characteristics: Represented by capital letters (A, B, T)
Only one copy needed for trait expression
classifications.behavior: Dominant alleles produce functional proteins or sufficient protein levels to express the trait even when only one copy is present; heterozygous individuals show the same phenotype as homozygous dominant individuals

examples.explanation: Brown eye allele (B) is dominant over blue eye allele (b); individuals with BB or Bb genotypes have brown eyes; only bb individuals have blue eyes

image.caption: Brown eye allele is dominant, expressing in both homozygous and heterozygous conditions.

examples.explanation: Widow's peak allele (W) is dominant over straight hairline (w); individuals with WW or Ww genotypes have widow's peak; only ww individuals have straight hairline

image.caption: Widow's peak is a classic example of dominant trait inheritance.

examples.explanation: Huntington's disease allele (H) is dominant; individuals with HH or Hh genotypes develop the disease; this is one of few dominant genetic disorders

image.caption: Huntington's disease demonstrates dominant inheritance in human genetic disorders.

classifications.value: Alleles that only express their trait in homozygous recessive (aa) genotype
characteristics: Represented by lowercase letters (a, b, t)
Masked by dominant allele in heterozygotes
Two copies needed for trait expression
Can skip generations when carried by heterozygotes
classifications.behavior: Recessive alleles often produce non-functional proteins or insufficient protein levels; trait only appears when no dominant allele is present to mask the recessive effect

examples.explanation: Blue eye allele (b) is recessive; only individuals with bb genotype have blue eyes; Bb individuals have brown eyes despite carrying blue allele

image.caption: Blue eye allele requires two copies for expression.

examples.explanation: Cystic fibrosis allele (f) is recessive; only ff individuals develop the disease; Ff carriers are healthy but can pass allele to offspring

image.caption: Cystic fibrosis demonstrates recessive inheritance in human genetic disorders.

examples.explanation: Attached earlobe allele (e) is recessive to free earlobe (E); only ee individuals have attached earlobes; EE and Ee individuals have free earlobes

image.caption: Attached earlobes are a classic recessive trait example.

classifications.value: Heterozygous individuals who carry one recessive allele but do not express the recessive trait
characteristics: Can pass recessive allele to offspring
classifications.behavior: Carriers appear normal for the trait but can transmit recessive alleles to offspring; when two carriers have children, 25% of offspring may express the recessive trait

examples.explanation: Individual with Ff genotype is healthy (dominant phenotype) but carries cystic fibrosis allele; can pass f allele to children

image.caption: Carriers are healthy but can transmit recessive disease alleles.

examples.explanation: Individual with AS genotype (one normal hemoglobin allele, one sickle cell allele) is generally healthy but can pass sickle cell allele to offspring

image.caption: Sickle cell trait carriers have one normal and one sickle cell allele.

principles.statement: In complete dominance, the dominant allele completely masks the recessive allele's expression in heterozygous individuals.
principles.application: Explains why heterozygous individuals show the same phenotype as homozygous dominant individuals; enables prediction of offspring phenotypes from parental genotypes.
principles.example: In pea plants, Pp heterozygotes show purple flowers identical to PP homozygotes; white flower allele (p) is completely masked.
principles.analogy: Like a loud speaker drowning out a quiet one—the dominant allele's effect is fully visible while the recessive allele's effect is hidden in heterozygotes.

image.alt: Complete dominance showing heterozygote matching homozygous dominant phenotype
image.caption: Complete dominance means heterozygotes express the dominant phenotype identically to homozygous dominants.

principles.statement: Recessive traits can appear to skip generations when carried by heterozygous individuals who do not express the trait.
principles.application: Explains family pedigree patterns where unaffected parents produce affected children; important for genetic counseling and risk assessment.
principles.example: Two brown-eyed parents (both Bb) can have a blue-eyed child (bb); the blue eye trait skipped the parent generation but reappeared in offspring.
principles.analogy: Like a secret message passed through generations—it remains hidden in carriers but can be revealed when two carriers have children together.

image.alt: Pedigree showing recessive trait skipping generations
image.caption: Recessive traits can skip generations when carried by heterozygous individuals.

principles.statement: Multiple genotypes can produce the same phenotype in dominant-recessive inheritance (AA and Aa both show dominant phenotype).
principles.application: Explains why phenotype alone cannot always determine genotype; test crosses are needed to distinguish homozygous dominant from heterozygous individuals.
principles.example: A purple-flowered pea plant could be PP or Pp; phenotype is the same but genotype differs, affecting what alleles it can pass to offspring.
principles.analogy: Like two packages with the same external label but different contents—same appearance (phenotype) but different genetic makeup (genotype).

image.caption: Different genotypes can produce identical phenotypes in dominant-recessive inheritance.

components.definition: Dominant-recessive inheritance involves allele interactions, genotype-phenotype relationships, and predictable inheritance patterns based on parental genotypes.
components.equation: $P(\text{recessive phenotype}) = P(\text{recessive allele from parent 1}) \times P(\text{recessive allele from parent 2})$
components.interpretation: For recessive trait to appear, offspring must inherit recessive allele from both parents; probability depends on each parent's genotype and gamete production.

image.caption: Dominant-recessive inheritance involves allele interactions, genotypes, and predictable phenotype ratios.

categories.description: Dominant traits located on non-sex chromosomes (autosomes) showing specific inheritance patterns
categories.detailed_mechanism: Affected individuals have at least one affected parent; trait appears in every generation; males and females equally affected; unaffected individuals do not transmit the trait

examples.explanation: Autosomal dominant disorder; affected parent (Hh) has 50% chance of passing disease allele to each child; disease appears in every generation where allele is present

image.caption: Autosomal dominant traits appear in every generation with affected individuals having affected parents.

examples.explanation: Autosomal dominant connective tissue disorder; affected individuals have 50% chance of passing to offspring; variable expression but dominant inheritance pattern

image.caption: Marfan syndrome demonstrates autosomal dominant inheritance with variable expression.
Autosomal dominant inheritance shows affected individuals in every generation.

categories.description: Recessive traits located on autosomes showing characteristic inheritance patterns with carrier parents
categories.detailed_mechanism: Affected individuals often have unaffected parents (both carriers); trait can skip generations; males and females equally affected; 25% risk when both parents are carriers

examples.explanation: Autosomal recessive disorder; carrier parents (Ff × Ff) have 25% chance of affected child (ff), 50% chance of carrier child (Ff), 25% chance of unaffected non-carrier (FF)

image.caption: Autosomal recessive traits can appear in children of unaffected carrier parents.

examples.explanation: Autosomal recessive blood disorder; carrier parents have 25% risk of affected child; heterozygote advantage in malaria-endemic regions maintains allele in population

image.alt: Sickle cell anemia autosomal recessive inheritance
image.caption: Sickle cell anemia demonstrates autosomal recessive inheritance with carrier parents.
Autosomal recessive inheritance shows affected individuals with unaffected carrier parents.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Offspring from Heterozygous Parents
worked_examples.problem: Two parents are both heterozygous for brown eyes (Bb × Bb), where brown (B) is dominant over blue (b). What is the probability their child will have blue eyes?

step_by_step.explanation: Identify parental genotypes: both parents are Bb (heterozygous brown eyes).
Determine gametes: each parent produces B and b gametes in equal proportions (50% each).
Construct Punnett square and identify blue eye genotype (bb requires recessive allele from both parents).
step_by_step.calculation: $P(bb) = P(b \text{ from parent 1}) \times P(b \text{ from parent 2}) = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{4}$

worked_examples.final_answer: The probability of blue eyes is 1/4 or 25%. Blue eyes only appear in homozygous recessive (bb) offspring, which occurs in 1 of 4 possible combinations.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Recessive traits require two recessive alleles; heterozygous parents each have 50% chance of passing recessive allele, producing 25% homozygous recessive offspring.

image.alt: Punnett square for Bb × Bb cross
image.caption: Heterozygous parent cross produces 25% recessive phenotype offspring.

worked_examples.title: Determining Parent Genotype from Offspring
worked_examples.problem: A brown-eyed man and a blue-eyed woman have a blue-eyed child. Brown eyes (B) are dominant over blue (b). What must be the father's genotype?

step_by_step.explanation: Identify known information: mother is blue-eyed (must be bb), child is blue-eyed (must be bb), father is brown-eyed (B_).
Analyze child's genotype: child received one b from mother (only option) and one b from father (required for bb genotype).
Conclude father's genotype: father must carry b allele to pass to child; since father has brown eyes, he must be Bb (heterozygous).

worked_examples.final_answer: The father must be heterozygous (Bb). He has brown eyes (dominant phenotype) but carries the blue eye allele that he passed to his blue-eyed child.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Offspring genotype reveals parental alleles; recessive offspring must receive recessive allele from each parent, revealing carrier status in dominant-phenotype parents.

image.caption: Offspring phenotypes can reveal hidden parental genotypes.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Carrier Risk in Families
worked_examples.problem: Two healthy parents have a child with cystic fibrosis (recessive disorder). What is the probability their next child will also have cystic fibrosis? What is the probability their next child will be a carrier?

step_by_step.explanation: Determine parental genotypes: affected child (ff) means both parents must be carriers (Ff).
Calculate probability of affected child: P(ff) = P(f from parent 1) × P(f from parent 2) = 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4.
Calculate probability of carrier child: P(Ff) = P(F from parent 1, f from parent 2) + P(f from parent 1, F from parent 2) = 1/4 + 1/4 = 1/2.

worked_examples.final_answer: Probability of next child having cystic fibrosis: 25%. Probability of next child being a carrier: 50%. Each pregnancy has independent probabilities; previous affected child does not change future probabilities.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Carrier parents have consistent probabilities for each pregnancy; recessive disorders show 25% affected, 50% carrier, 25% unaffected non-carrier ratios.

image.caption: Carrier parents have predictable probabilities for each pregnancy outcome.

components.composition: Alleles that express trait in both homozygous dominant (AA) and heterozygous (Aa) genotypes
components.function: Determine phenotype when present; mask recessive allele expression in heterozygotes
components.key_feature: Only one copy needed for trait expression; represented by capital letters

image.alt: Dominant allele expression in different genotypes
image.caption: Dominant alleles express in both homozygous and heterozygous conditions.

components.composition: Alleles that only express trait in homozygous recessive (aa) genotype
components.function: Remain hidden in heterozygotes; express only when no dominant allele is present
components.key_feature: Two copies needed for trait expression; can skip generations when carried by heterozygotes

image.alt: Recessive allele expression requiring homozygous condition
image.caption: Recessive alleles require two copies for phenotypic expression.

components.composition: Individuals with one dominant and one recessive allele (Aa)
components.function: Appear normal for trait but can transmit recessive allele to offspring
components.key_feature: Phenotypically dominant but genotypically carry recessive allele; critical in recessive disorder transmission

image.caption: Carriers appear normal but can transmit recessive alleles to offspring.

function.operation: Dominant-recessive inheritance functions through allele interactions during fertilization, where offspring genotype determines phenotype based on dominance relationships.
function.energy: No biological energy required for inheritance pattern itself; represents molecular interaction between allele products determining trait expression
function.mechanism: Dominant alleles produce functional proteins or sufficient protein levels; recessive alleles often produce non-functional proteins; heterozygotes have enough functional protein from one dominant allele to express dominant phenotype

image.alt: Molecular mechanism of dominant-recessive allele interaction
image.caption: Dominant-recessive inheritance reflects molecular interactions between allele products.

cycle.operation: Parents pass one allele per gene to offspring through gametes during fertilization
cycle.purpose: Transmit genetic information to next generation; create new allele combinations

image.alt: Allele transmission from parents to offspring
image.caption: Allele transmission creates offspring genotypes from parental alleles.

cycle.operation: Offspring receive one allele from each parent, forming homozygous or heterozygous genotype
cycle.purpose: Establish genetic makeup that will determine phenotype based on dominance relationships

image.caption: Genotype formation combines parental alleles into offspring genetic makeup.

cycle.operation: Genotype determines phenotype through dominant-recessive allele interactions at molecular level
cycle.purpose: Produce observable traits based on which alleles are present and their dominance relationships

image.caption: Phenotype expression reflects genotype and dominance relationships.

process_flow.description: Determine which trait is being studied and which allele is dominant versus recessive.
process_flow.mechanism: Review trait information; assign letter symbols (capital for dominant, lowercase for recessive); confirm complete dominance relationship applies.
process_flow.result: Clear allele symbols and dominance relationship established for inheritance analysis.

image.caption: Clear dominance relationship identification is essential for accurate inheritance prediction.

process_flow.description: Establish the exact genotype of each parent based on phenotype and family history.
process_flow.mechanism: Dominant phenotype may be homozygous or heterozygous; recessive phenotype must be homozygous recessive; use offspring information or test cross if genotype uncertain.
process_flow.result: Parental genotypes confirmed for accurate offspring probability calculation.

image.caption: Parental genotype determination enables accurate offspring prediction.

process_flow.description: Use Punnett square or probability rules to predict possible offspring genotypes.
process_flow.mechanism: Combine parental gametes systematically; calculate probabilities for each genotype based on allele segregation during gamete formation.
process_flow.result: Complete list of possible offspring genotypes with their probabilities.

image.caption: Offspring genotype prediction uses parental gamete combinations.

process_flow.description: Apply dominance relationships to convert genotypes to phenotypes.
process_flow.mechanism: Homozygous dominant and heterozygous genotypes express dominant phenotype; only homozygous recessive expresses recessive phenotype.
process_flow.result: Predicted phenotype ratios for offspring (e.g., 3:1 for heterozygote cross).

image.caption: Phenotype determination applies dominance relationships to genotypes.

process_flow.description: Express predictions as probabilities, percentages, or ratios for practical application.
process_flow.mechanism: Count genotype/phenotype combinations; calculate probabilities; express as ratios (3:1), percentages (75%), or fractions (3/4).
process_flow.result: Quantitative predictions for genetic counseling, breeding decisions, or research planning.

image.caption: Probability calculations convert genetic predictions into actionable information.

process_types.energy_profile: Analytical process; direction: family history → inheritance pattern identification
process_types.direction: Pedigree patterns reveal whether trait is dominant or recessive based on family distribution
process_types.function: Enables identification of inheritance pattern from family history without knowing specific genotypes

examples.explanation: Affected individuals in every generation; affected children have at least one affected parent; unaffected individuals do not transmit trait; males and females equally affected

image.caption: Dominant traits appear in every generation with affected individuals having affected parents.

examples.explanation: Trait can skip generations; affected children often have unaffected parents (carriers); males and females equally affected; more common in consanguineous families

image.caption: Recessive traits can skip generations with affected children having unaffected carrier parents.

image.alt: Comparison of dominant and recessive pedigree patterns
image.caption: Pedigree patterns distinguish dominant from recessive inheritance.

process_types.energy_profile: Breeding/analytical process; direction: unknown genotype → known genotype through offspring analysis
process_types.direction: Test cross with homozygous recessive reveals whether dominant phenotype individual is homozygous or heterozygous
process_types.function: Determines unknown genotype of dominant phenotype individuals for breeding or counseling purposes

examples.explanation: Purple-flowered plant (P_) crossed with white-flowered (pp); all purple offspring indicates PP parent; 1:1 purple:white indicates Pp parent

image.caption: Test crosses reveal unknown plant genotypes for breeding decisions.

examples.explanation: Healthy animal with dominant phenotype crossed with recessive individual; offspring ratios reveal whether healthy animal is carrier; important for eliminating genetic disorders from breeding lines

image.caption: Test crosses identify carriers in animal breeding programs.
Test crosses determine unknown genotypes in various genetic applications.

sections.definition: Dominant-recessive inheritance describes allele interactions where dominant alleles mask recessive alleles in heterozygotes.
sections.purpose: To understand how dominant and recessive alleles interact to determine trait expression, enable prediction of inheritance patterns, and apply this knowledge to genetic counseling, breeding, and medical genetics.

activation.definition: Dominant-recessive inheritance is activated during fertilization when parental alleles combine to form offspring genotype, with phenotype determined by dominance relationships.
activation.effect: Offspring phenotype reflects dominance relationships: dominant alleles express in heterozygotes, recessive alleles only express in homozygous recessive condition.

image.alt: Activation of dominant-recessive inheritance during fertilization
image.caption: Dominant-recessive inheritance is activated when parental alleles combine in offspring.

key_properties.explanation: Standard dominant-recessive patterns assume complete dominance where heterozygotes match homozygous dominant phenotype; incomplete dominance and codominance produce different patterns covered in Section 9.8.

image.alt: Complete dominance vs other inheritance patterns
image.caption: Complete dominance is the standard pattern for simple Mendelian traits.

key_properties.explanation: Each parent passes one allele per gene to offspring with equal probability (50% for each allele in heterozygotes); forms basis for probability calculations in genetic predictions.

image.alt: Equal allele segregation during gamete formation
image.caption: Equal segregation enables accurate probability predictions.

key_properties.explanation: Different genes assort independently (when on different chromosomes); dominant-recessive relationship for one gene does not affect inheritance of other genes.

image.caption: Different genes inherit independently when on different chromosomes.

key_properties.explanation: Understanding dominant-recessive inheritance enables genetic counseling for inherited disorders, breeding program design in agriculture, and risk assessment for families with genetic conditions.

image.caption: Dominant-recessive inheritance knowledge applies to medicine, agriculture, and genetic counseling.

summary: Dominant and recessive inheritance describes how alleles interact to determine trait expression. Dominant alleles (capital letters) express their trait in both homozygous dominant (AA) and heterozygous (Aa) genotypes, while recessive alleles (lowercase) only express in homozygous recessive (aa) genotypes. Key principles include: complete dominance (heterozygotes match homozygous dominant phenotype), recessive traits can skip generations when carried by heterozygotes, and multiple genotypes can produce same phenotype. Autosomal dominant inheritance shows affected individuals in every generation with at least one affected parent; autosomal recessive inheritance shows affected children often with unaffected carrier parents (25% risk when both parents carriers). Carriers are heterozygous individuals who appear normal but can transmit recessive alleles. Applications include pedigree analysis to identify inheritance patterns, test crosses to determine unknown genotypes, and genetic counseling for inherited disorders. Understanding dominant-recessive inheritance enables prediction of offspring phenotypes, assessment of genetic risks, and informed decisions in breeding and medical contexts. This foundation prepares for more complex inheritance patterns including incomplete dominance, codominance, and polygenic traits covered in Section 9.8.
